description: Boss literature translated into deployable Risk Manager Platform modules.

modules.name: WorldQuant Alpha Signal Lab
key_takeaways: Formulaic alphas are explicit code-like signals, mostly built from price, volume, VWAP, ranks, delays, correlations, rolling statistics, and industry neutralization.
The paper distinguishes delay-0 and delay-1 signals, which is critical for no-look-ahead backtesting and execution timing.
The alphas have short holding periods, low average pairwise correlation, and require alpha portfolio construction rather than one-signal trading.
Turnover alone has weak explanatory power for alpha correlations, but turnover and volatility still matter for costs, capacity, and specific risk.
implementation_requirements: Build a formulaic alpha operator library: rank, delay, delta, correlation, covariance, ts_rank, ts_min, ts_max, decay_linear, scale, signedpower, industry neutralization.
Create an alpha registry that records source, delay, input fields, holding horizon, turnover expectation, neutralization requirement, and data availability.
Backtest selected alphas with strict signal timestamping, transaction costs, turnover, capacity, and alpha correlation diagnostics.
Combine validated alphas into diversified alpha baskets rather than trading a single alpha.
dashboard_requirements: Alpha Signal Lab table with alpha family, delay, required data, validation status, IC, turnover, net Sharpe, and correlation to active strategies.
Alpha correlation heatmap and turnover/cost panel.
No-look-ahead checklist for every formulaic signal.

modules.name: Hedge Fund Replication Clone Strategy
source_files: Hedge Fund Replication - Can Hedge Fund Returns be Replicated.pdf
key_takeaways: Hedge fund returns can be decomposed into common risk factor premia and manager-specific alpha.
The paper uses six broad factors: USD, bonds, credit spread, S&P 500, commodities, and change in VIX.
Clone portfolios can be built with liquid instruments using fixed-weight or rolling-window regressions.
Rolling-window clones reduce look-ahead bias but introduce estimation error and higher rebalancing needs.
Replication works better for some styles such as Global Macro, Managed Futures, Long/Short Equity, and Fund of Funds than for illiquidity-heavy styles such as Event Driven and Emerging Markets.
implementation_requirements: Create a hedge fund replication strategy that regresses target strategy or hedge fund style returns on liquid factor proxies.
Use ETF/futures proxies for SPY, bonds, USD, credit, commodities, and volatility change.
Support fixed-weight clone research for explanation only and rolling-window clone for deployable no-look-ahead testing.
Track beta premia contribution, residual alpha, R-squared, autocorrelation, and liquidity gap.
dashboard_requirements: Replication panel showing factor betas, replicated return, residual return, R-squared, and rolling clone weights.
Clone vs target return distribution and drawdown comparison.
Warning flag when target strategy likely earns illiquidity premium that liquid clones cannot replicate.

modules.name: JPM Regime-Aware Asset Allocation
key_takeaways: No single asset class dominates across all economic regimes.
Regime-based allocation should be aware of growth, inflation, monetary policy, and labor market slack.
Asset responses are state-dependent and non-linear, so simple linear relationships can miss regime effects.
Regime investing requires economic foresight, but the paper argues even imperfect directional foresight can add value.
The framework should adjust around strategic benchmarks rather than abandon benchmark discipline.
implementation_requirements: Add a macro regime classifier using growth, inflation, policy, labor, volatility, credit, and liquidity proxies.
Create regime-specific asset class expected-return and risk assumptions.
Add allocation tilts around strategic weights, with constraints and human approval.
Model regime confidence and require a confidence threshold before changing allocations.
dashboard_requirements: Current regime label, regime confidence, key supporting indicators, and previous regime.
Current vs regime-aware proposed allocation with risk and cost impact.

modules.name: Markov Regime Switching Risk Engine
source_files: Markov Regimes - How Regimes Affect Asset Allocation - By Ang and Bekaert.pdf
key_takeaways: International equity returns exhibit normal and high-volatility bear regimes.
High-volatility bear regimes tend to have lower expected returns and higher cross-asset correlations.
Markov transition probabilities allow regimes to be persistent but uncertain.
Regime-dependent asset allocation can shift portfolios toward lower-volatility assets or cash when bear/high-vol regimes are likely.
Practical implementation must account for transaction costs, constraints, tracking error, and higher-order moments.
implementation_requirements: Prototype a two-state Markov regime model using equity returns, volatility, correlation, short rates, and drawdown proxies.
Output regime probabilities rather than hard labels only.
Use regime probabilities to adjust covariance, correlation, and risk-budget assumptions.
Add transaction costs and turnover controls to any regime-switching allocation.
dashboard_requirements: Regime probability chart, transition probability summary, and high-vol correlation warning.
Normal-regime vs stress-regime covariance and correlation views.
Defensive allocation proposal when high-vol probability crosses review threshold.

modules.name: Business Cycle Dynamic Allocation
key_takeaways: Valuation is more useful over long horizons, while macro cycle indicators are more useful over short and medium horizons.
The paper defines four macro regimes using level of growth versus trend and change in growth: recovery, expansion, slowdown, and contraction.
Leading indicators are preferred over lagged GDP or unemployment because they are more timely and less revised.
Risk assets tend to perform best when growth accelerates; government bonds tend to perform best in contraction.
Slowdown regimes often have unattractive risk-adjusted returns and call for neutral or cautious allocation.
implementation_requirements: Build a business-cycle regime classifier based on leading indicators, market proxies, and growth acceleration/deceleration.
Map regimes to risk premia: equity, high yield, investment grade credit, duration, and cash.
Separate long-term valuation signals from medium-term macro regime signals.
Create regime fit scores for each strategy and allocation sleeve.
dashboard_requirements: Business cycle clock: recovery, expansion, slowdown, contraction.
Strategy regime fit table with favorable, neutral, and unfavorable states.
Allocation recommendation reason code: macro regime tilt, risk reduction, or neutral stance.

modules.name: QuantFin Implementation Stack
key_takeaways: Boss document points to Qlib, NautilusTrader, TradingAgents, Kronos, volatility surface modeling, crypto arbitrage, and prediction-market tooling.
For this platform, Qlib is the best near-term research/backtest engine candidate; NautilusTrader is more relevant later for event-driven execution architecture.
AI agents and LLM tools should support research, monitoring, and explanation, not authorize trades.
Volatility surface and crypto/Polymarket arbitrage ideas should remain research modules until data, execution, and risk controls are clearly specified.
implementation_requirements: Keep the current platform as a risk-manager workstation, not an execution system.
Use Qlib-style experiment management for alpha and backtest research where appropriate.
Define future adapters for NautilusTrader only after strategy specs and execution assumptions exist.
Treat AI agent outputs as human-review research notes with source, timestamp, and confidence.
dashboard_requirements: Research source tracker for boss-provided tools and papers.
Implementation status by module: intake, prototype, backtest, WFO, live monitor, approved.
Human-review and data-lineage flags for AI-generated research outputs.
